project.name: tiki-v2
project.description: Tiki v2 is a GitHub-issue-centric workflow framework for Claude Code that orchestrates software development against GitHub issues through a structured GET to REVIEW to PLAN to AUDIT to EXECUTE to SHIP pipeline. It ships as a pnpm monorepo pairing a Claude Code command plugin and shared TypeScript types with a Tauri desktop app (React frontend, Rust backend) for visualizing and driving that workflow.

nodes.summary: GitHub Actions PR workflow that runs the full CI gate on pull requests to main: pnpm typecheck, build, Vitest (shared + desktop), cargo test, and clippy with --deny warnings.
nodes.languageNotes: Single ubuntu-22.04 job installing Tauri system libs; concurrency group cancels in-progress runs per ref.

nodes.languageNotes: Matrix build across 4 platform targets; uses tauri-apps/tauri-action with signing keys and updater JSON.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the README to understand what Tiki v2 is: a GitHub-issue-centric workflow framework for Claude Code that drives a structured GET → REVIEW → PLAN → AUDIT → EXECUTE → SHIP pipeline. It introduces the two products that share this repo and a version number — the Tiki Framework (a Claude Code plugin) and Tiki Desktop (a Tauri app) — and the four architecture principles (GitHub as source of truth, fresh-context execution, single state file, multi-context support). CLAUDE.md is the companion orientation doc, repeating the monorepo layout and the .tiki state model that the rest of the tour explores.

tour.title: Architecture Deep Dive
tour.description: Before reading code, skim the design documents that lay out how the whole system fits together. DESIGN.md is the 773-line canonical architecture covering the pipeline, the .tiki state model, the command-file format, and the lifecycle-hook system; FRAMEWORK-DEEP-DIVE.md walks the same pipeline from the framework's perspective and connects it to the @tiki/shared type system and the desktop app. These give you the mental model that every later step grounds itself in.

tour.title: The Workflow Pipeline Commands
tour.description: The framework product IS the pipeline, and each stage is a Markdown+XML command file the Claude Code plugin registers. Read these in pipeline order — get (fetch issue), review (analyze), plan (break into phases), audit (validate the plan forms a DAG with full success-criteria coverage), execute (run phases via sub-agents), ship (test, commit, push, close) — to see how a GitHub issue flows from intake to merged code. These files are the conceptual entry point of the framework: they orchestrate everything else.

tour.title: Automation: YOLO & Release
tour.description: Building on the per-stage commands from Step 3, two higher-level commands chain them into full automation. yolo.md runs the entire GET→SHIP pipeline end-to-end with explicit per-step state-shim invocations so no transition is dropped; release.md orchestrates every issue in a release in dependency order (wave-based DAG) before tagging and shipping. Together they show how the atomic pipeline steps compose into the hands-off cascades that drive Tiki's actual releases.

tour.title: The State Shim — Heart of the Framework
tour.description: Every command in Steps 3–4 mutates workflow state through one file: state.mjs, a bash-callable Node CLI (the #3 most depended-upon node by fan-in). It validates that a status transition is legal, holds a cross-process file lock around the read-modify-write, and writes atomically via temp-file+rename — the discipline that keeps .tiki/state.json consistent across many concurrent terminals. run-hook.mjs is its sibling: the lifecycle-hook runner the execute and ship commands fire at six points, where pre-* hooks block the pipeline on failure and post-*/phase-* hooks only warn. HOOKS.md documents that contract.
tour.languageLesson: These scripts use only Node built-ins (no dependencies) so they run anywhere the plugin is installed. The atomic write pattern — write to a temp file, then rename — is a POSIX/Win32 trick: rename is atomic, so a reader never sees a half-written state.json even if the process crashes mid-write.

tour.title: Shared Types & the Transition Table
tour.description: The state shim from Step 5 doesn't define the rules of legal state changes by itself — it mirrors them from @tiki/shared. state.ts declares the core vocabulary: WorkStatus/PhaseStatus and the IssueWork | ReleaseWork discriminated union that models every work item. transitions.ts holds VALID_TRANSITIONS, the single canonical table of which status can follow which, plus the canTransition/assertTransition guards. This table is the contract that BOTH the state.mjs shim AND the Rust backend (Step 8) independently enforce.
tour.languageLesson: TypeScript discriminated unions (IssueWork | ReleaseWork sharing a literal 'type' field) let the compiler narrow to the exact variant after a type check. CLAUDE.md notes a gotcha: narrowing through an intermediate boolean fails under `tsc -b` even though it passes `tsc --noEmit` — which is why the project verifies with the stricter build.

tour.title: Runtime Validation & JSON Schemas
tour.description: Types vanish at runtime, so @tiki/shared also ships Ajv-based validators and the JSON Schemas (draft 2020-12) that describe the on-disk files. validation/index.ts exposes validate*/assertValid* helpers (with unknown-key collection) used across both products, while state.schema.json, plan.schema.json, and config.schema.json formally define state.json, the per-issue plan files, and .tiki/config.json. This is the safety net that catches malformed state the type system can't.
tour.languageLesson: JSON Schema draft 2020-12 with Ajv validates data structurally at runtime. Setting additionalProperties handling carefully (collect unknown keys as warnings rather than hard-failing) lets the schema evolve without breaking older state files — a pragmatic forward-compatibility choice.

tour.title: Desktop Backend — Tauri Bootstrap & State Mirror
tour.description: Now switch to the second product. The Rust/Tauri backend boots from main.rs (a thin binary) into lib.rs, the crate root that registers every backend module and exposes ~30 IPC commands via the invoke_handler — making it the natural BFS entry point of the backend. commands.rs is that IPC surface the React frontend calls. Crucially, state_transition.rs is the typed Rust mirror of the same transition contract from Step 6, and state.rs defines the serde data model (with lenient deserializers for legacy state.json shapes) — so the desktop app and the framework shim enforce identical rules from two languages.
tour.languageLesson: Tauri splits a desktop app into a Rust core and a web frontend bridged by IPC: functions tagged #[tauri::command] become callable from JavaScript via invoke(). serde's lenient deserializers (custom impls / #[serde(default)]) let one Rust struct read several historical JSON shapes — how state.rs absorbs legacy formats without migrations.

tour.title: Watching .tiki & Filesystem Safety
tour.description: The desktop app stays live because watcher.rs monitors the active project's .tiki/ directory and forwards typed file events (state/plan/release/research) to the frontend, using trailing-edge debounce so the final write of a burst always lands. fs_utils.rs underpins it and the IPC layer with resilient JSON reads, the same atomic temp-file+rename writes used by the shim, plus backup/restore with retention pruning. github.rs is the integration layer that shells out to the gh CLI to fetch and mutate issues — the bridge that makes 'GitHub as source of truth' real on the desktop side.

tour.title: The Embedded Terminal (PTY)
tour.description: A standout backend feature is the integrated terminal, which lets you run /tiki commands without leaving the app. The terminal module (mod.rs barrel) exposes IPC commands (commands.rs) to create, write, resize, and destroy sessions, while pty.rs spawns real shells through portable-pty, tracks them in a global TerminalManager, and streams batched UTF-8 output to the frontend over Tauri events. This is what the React xterm.js component in Step 13 connects to.

tour.title: Frontend Boot — React Shell
tour.description: Crossing into the UI layer: main.tsx mounts the root component with React's createRoot, and App.tsx is the application shell (the #1 node by fan-out, importing 32 modules). App.tsx is where the architecture converges — it loads Tiki state, wires the Rust watcher's .tiki file events (Step 9) into the Zustand stores, schedules GitHub refreshes, and runs global shortcuts and the bulk-YOLO advance loop. Reading it shows how backend events become reactive frontend updates.

tour.title: Frontend State — Zustand Stores
tour.description: The watcher events App.tsx receives flow into Zustand stores, the frontend's state management layer. The stores barrel (index.ts) is the single most depended-upon file in the whole graph (fan-in 41) — components import the entire state layer from it. tikiStateStore.ts is the single source for parsed state.json that sidebar, kanban, and detail views consume; terminalStore.ts (the largest store) manages tabs, split-pane layout, and the work-to-terminal mapping. deriveDisplayStatus.ts is the pure selector that resolves a work item's effective status via a first-match precedence table — the single answer every view uses for placement and badges.
tour.languageLesson: Zustand stores expose state via selector hooks backed by React 18/19's useSyncExternalStore. A subtle trap (which this project hit and fixed) is returning a fresh object/array from a selector on every call — React then sees a new reference each render and loops; hoisting stable empty constants to module scope avoids it.

tour.title: Key Feature Views
tour.description: Finally, the views that make the state visible and actionable. KanbanBoard.tsx groups issues into pipeline columns and turns drag-and-drop into execute/ship commands. IssueDetail.tsx is the primary detail panel showing metadata, plan phases, the pipeline timeline, and step actions. The dependency graph (DependencyGraph.tsx with the useDependencyGraph.ts hook) renders a release's issues as a React Flow DAG with critical-path and swimlane layout. Terminal.tsx is the xterm.js component wired to the Rust PTY from Step 10. These views consume the stores from Step 12 and the deriveDisplayStatus selector for a consistent picture.

tour.title: CI/CD — Test & Release Gate
tour.description: As a capstone, see how all of the above ships safely. pr.yml runs the full CI gate on every pull request to main — pnpm typecheck, the stricter build, Vitest for shared and desktop, cargo test, and clippy with --deny warnings — the exact gate referenced throughout CLAUDE.md. release.yml fires on a v* tag push to cross-build the Tauri binary on macOS/Ubuntu/Windows, sync the version from the tag, compose notes from the per-release changelog, and publish signed installers. This closes the loop from the SHIP command in Step 3 to a downloadable release.
tour.languageLesson: GitHub Actions trigger on `on:` events — pr.yml on pull_request, release.yml on `push: tags: 'v*'`. The release workflow only runs after a tag is pushed, so nothing appears in the Actions dashboard until the tag exists — a real gotcha when expecting a build to start at merge time.
